examples.title: Check 1NF Compliance
examples.description: Algorithm to check if a relation is in First Normal Form
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Check1NF(Relation R)
BEGIN
  FOR EACH attribute A IN R DO
    FOR EACH tuple t IN R DO
      IF value(t.A) is atomic THEN
        CONTINUE
      ELSE
        RETURN FALSE, "Attribute " + A + " has non-atomic values"
      END IF
    END FOR
  END FOR
  RETURN TRUE, "Relation is in 1NF"
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm checks each attribute in every tuple to ensure all values are atomic (single, indivisible values). If any attribute contains composite or multi-valued data (like arrays, sets, or comma-separated values), it violates 1NF.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × m) where n = tuples, m = attributes

examples.title: Find Candidate Keys
examples.description: Algorithm to find all candidate keys from functional dependencies
examples.code:
ALGORITHM FindCandidateKeys(Attributes set A, FDs set F)
BEGIN
  // Compute closure of single attributes
  candidateKeys = []
  FOR EACH attr x IN A DO
    closure = ComputeClosure(x, F)
    IF closure == A THEN
      candidateKeys.append(x)
    END IF
  END FOR
  
  // If no single key, try combinations
  IF candidateKeys is empty THEN
    FOR size = 2 TO |A| DO
      FOR EACH combination combo of size FROM A DO
        closure = ComputeClosure(combo, F)
        IF closure == A AND NOT isRedundant(combo, candidateKeys) THEN
          candidateKeys.append(combo)
        END IF
      END FOR
    END FOR
  END IF
  
  RETURN candidateKeys
END

ALGORITHM ComputeClosure(X, FDs)
BEGIN
  result = X
  REPEAT
    changed = FALSE
    FOR EACH FD Y→Z IN FDs DO
      IF Y ⊆ result AND Z ⊄ result THEN
        result = result ∪ Z
        changed = TRUE
      END IF
    END FOR
  UNTIL changed == FALSE
  RETURN result
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm finds all candidate keys by first checking if single attributes can determine all others, then checking combinations of attributes. The closure computation is key - an attribute set is a candidate key if its closure equals the entire attribute set and it is minimal (no subset is a key).
examples.timeComplexity: O(2^n × m) exponential in number of attributes

examples.title: Normalize to 2NF - Remove Partial Dependencies
examples.code:
def normalize_to_2nf(relation_name, attributes, fds, candidate_keys):
    '''
    Decompose relation to 2NF by removing partial dependencies
    '''
    # Step 1: Identify partial dependencies
    # A partial dependency exists when non-prime attribute 
    # depends on part of a composite candidate key
    
    prime_attributes = set()
    for key in candidate_keys:
        prime_attributes.update(key)
    
    non_prime_attributes = set(attributes) - prime_attributes
    
    # Find FDs that are partial (determinant is part of candidate key)
    partial_dependencies = []
    full_dependencies = []
    
    for determinant, dependent in fds:
        if set(determinant).issubset(prime_attributes):
            if not set(dependent).issubset(prime_attributes):
                partial_dependencies.append((determinant, dependent))
        else:
            full_dependencies.append((determinant, dependent))
    
    # Step 2: Create new relations for partial dependencies
    decomposed_relations = []
    
    for det, dep in partial_dependencies:
        new_attrs = set(det) | set(dep)
        decomposed_relations.append({
            'name': f"{relation_name}_{det[0]}",
            'attributes': new_attrs,
            'primary_key': det,
            'fds': [(det, dep)]
        })
    
    # Step 3: Create relation for remaining attributes
    remaining_attrs = set(attributes)
    for det, dep in partial_dependencies:
        remaining_attrs -= set(dep)
    
    if remaining_attrs:
        decomposed_relations.append({
            'name': relation_name,
            'attributes': remaining_attrs,
            'primary_key': candidate_keys[0],  # original key
            'fds': full_dependencies
        })
    
    return decomposed_relations

# Example usage:
# Relation: R(A, B, C, D)
# FDs: AB→CD, B→C
# Candidate key: AB
# This has partial dependency B→C (B is part of key AB)
# Result: R1(B, C) and R2(A, B, D)
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates decomposition to 2NF. It identifies partial dependencies where a non-prime attribute depends only on part of a candidate key. The algorithm creates separate relations for each partial dependency, preserving the full functional dependencies in the remaining relation.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × m) where n = number of FDs, m = number of attributes

examples.title: Check BCNF Compliance
examples.description: C program to check if relation satisfies BCNF
examples.explanation: This C program checks if a relation satisfies BCNF by verifying that every functional dependency's determinant is a superkey. In the example, B→C violates BCNF because B alone is not a candidate key (the key is AB). The algorithm compares each determinant against the full attribute set to determine if it can uniquely identify all tuples.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × m) where n = number of FDs, m = total attributes
